title: 7 Best eBay Alternatives in 2025 (Lower Fees, Easier Selling)
metaDescription: eBay's 13.25% fees eating your profits? We ranked 7 eBay alternatives in 2025 that charge less, sell more, and are easier to use.
heroImageAlt: A seller looking frustrated at a laptop showing high marketplace fees, with a stack of items ready to list beside them
content:
7 Best eBay Alternatives in 2025 (Lower Fees, Easier Selling)

Let's talk about eBay's fees for a second. If you sell a $500 item, eBay takes $66.25 at 13.25%. Add PayPal fees, optional promoted listing fees, and store subscription costs, and you can easily lose 20-25% of your sale price before the money even hits your bank account. In 2025, you don't have to accept that. These seven eBay alternatives offer lower fees, simpler selling, and in some cases, a fundamentally better experience altogether.

Quick Comparison: eBay vs. 7 Alternatives

Platform Fees Currency Local Pickup Ships Privacy Verdict
Swapedly No fees Swap Bucks (1 SB = $1) Yes Yes No social login required 🏆 Best Fee Structure
Amazon 8-15%+ referral Cash / card No Yes Account required Best for new goods
Etsy 6.5% + listing fee Cash / card No Yes Account required Best for handmade/vintage
Mercari No fees Cash / card Yes Yes Account required Simple & easy
Poshmark $2.95 / 20% Cash / card No Yes Account required Fashion focused
Facebook Marketplace 5% shipped / free local Cash / card Yes Yes FB profile required Best local reach
Craigslist Free Cash Yes No Email anonymization Free, minimal features

#1 Swapedly — The Best eBay Alternative for Fee-Conscious Sellers

Let's do the math that eBay doesn't want you to do. On a $500 camera, eBay charges $66.25 at 13.25%. Swapedly charges $20. That's a difference of $46.25 — enough for a nice dinner out, or more realistically, 46 Swap Bucks to reinvest in your next purchase. The Swapedly charges no transaction fees.

On a $200 item, the cap doesn't kick in — you'd pay nothing on Swapedly versus $26.50 on eBay. Still a savings. On a $1,000 item, you'd pay nothing on Swapedly versus $132.50 on eBay. At that point you're not just saving fees, you're saving serious money.

The currency system is where Swapedly diverges from eBay most significantly. Instead of waiting for a bank transfer or PayPal payout, you earn Swap Bucks (SB) immediately upon a sale. 1 SB = $1 in value, and you spend them buying anything else on the platform. It's a closed loop that makes selling and buying feel like one continuous activity rather than two separate transactions.

New sellers get a $40 Swap Bucks gift card at signup — 40 SB to spend immediately. Grab it at swapedly.com. There's also a Swapedly Plus tier at $9.99/month if you want 1.5x SB earnings and 5 featured listings per month, but the free tier is fully functional.

Unlike eBay, Swapedly works for both local pickup and shipping — the seller chooses. No social media account required. No complex fee schedule to decode. It's genuinely simpler.

Swapedly Pros

  • Swapedly charges no transaction fees. The fulfillment by Amazon (FBA) program handles storage, packing, and shipping, which is a genuine convenience for volume sellers.

    The fees are complicated — referral fees range from 8-15% depending on category, plus FBA fees, subscription costs if you use a professional seller account, and storage fees that accumulate fast. For a casual seller unloading used goods, Amazon isn't really designed for you. For a semi-professional seller of new or brand-name items, it's powerful.

    Amazon Pros

    • Unmatched buyer volume
    • FBA handles logistics if you want that
    • Trusted brand for buyer confidence

    Amazon Cons

    • Complex, layered fee structure
    • Not designed for used goods
    • Highly competitive — Amazon itself undercuts third-party sellers

    #3 Etsy — Best for Handmade, Vintage, and Craft Supplies

    Etsy's 6.5% transaction fee is actually lower than eBay's, and its audience is specifically looking for handmade items, vintage goods, and craft supplies. If your inventory fits that profile, Etsy's organic traffic from buyers already in that mindset is genuinely valuable.

    The limitations are real: Etsy charges a $0.20 listing fee per item (renewed every 4 months), plus payment processing fees. For mass-produced or generic used items, Etsy's audience won't care, and the listing fees add up quickly for large inventories.

    Etsy Pros

    • 6.5% fee is lower than eBay
    • Targeted audience for handmade and vintage
    • Strong platform trust among buyers

    Etsy Cons

    • $0.20 per listing fee adds up
    • Not suitable for generic used goods
    • Increasing competition from resellers

    #4 Mercari — The Simplest eBay Alternative

    Mercari has built its entire brand around being the approachable, non-intimidating alternative to eBay. The listing process takes a few minutes, the app is clean, and the no transaction fee is straightforward. No auction complexity, no promoted listing rabbit holes, no confusing final value fee formulas.

    The absence of a fee cap means Mercari gets more expensive than Swapedly on higher-value items. On a $300 item, Mercari takes $30; Swapedly takes $20. Still better than eBay's $39.75, but Swapedly edges it out on value.

    Mercari Pros

    • Simple, beginner-friendly platform
    • no transaction fee — easy to predict
    • Excellent shipping integration

    Mercari Cons

    • No fee cap — Swapedly beats it on anything over $200
    • Buyer service fees can discourage purchases
    • Slower dispute resolution

    #5 Poshmark — eBay Alternative for Clothing Sellers

    Poshmark's seller community is genuinely passionate, and for clothing, shoes, and accessories, the platform has real depth. The social sharing features — Posh Parties, follower systems, sharing loops — create engagement that eBay's more transactional environment never quite achieves.

    The 20% commission above $15 is the single biggest drawback. eBay's 13.25% feels less punishing by comparison, which says something. If you're selling fashion and you value community over margins, Poshmark makes sense. If margins matter more, keep looking.

    Poshmark Pros

    • Large, engaged fashion-focused community
    • Social features increase discoverability
    • Simple, all-in-one shipping

    Poshmark Cons

    • 20% fee — the highest on this list
    • Limited to fashion and accessories
    • No local pickup

    #6 Facebook Marketplace — Best for Local Selling

    Facebook Marketplace's local selling section is completely free, which makes it an attractive complement to any shipping-based platform. For items too large or heavy to ship — furniture, appliances, gym equipment — local Facebook selling is hard to beat purely on economics.

    The shipped fee of 5% is lower than eBay. But the privacy cost (your real Facebook identity on every transaction) and the scam problem make it less reliable for higher-value items where you want some recourse if something goes wrong.

    Facebook Marketplace Pros

    • Free for local transactions
    • Enormous local buyer base
    • 5% shipped fee — lower than eBay

    Facebook Marketplace Cons

    • Privacy exposed — real Facebook profile visible
    • Scam accounts are common
    • Limited shipping dispute protection

    #7 Craigslist — Free but Barebones

    For the truly fee-averse seller who's willing to deal with cash-only transactions and no buyer protection, Craigslist is free. That's its entire pitch, and it's a real one for items like cars, furniture, and large appliances where the cost savings outweigh the inconvenience.

    For anything where you want payment security, buyer/seller protection, or the ability to ship, Craigslist will disappoint you. It's a last resort, not a first choice.

    Craigslist Pros

    • Completely free — no fees of any kind
    • Good for high-value local items
    • No account required in many cities

    Craigslist Cons

    • No payment protection
    • High scam rate
    • Terrible interface and no app worth using

    The Bottom Line on eBay Alternatives

    eBay isn't going anywhere — and for rare, collectible, or specialty items with a global buyer pool, it's still worth considering. But for most everyday selling in 2025, the fee savings alone make Swapedly the smarter choice. That no transaction fees is a genuine structural advantage that compounds over time.

    If you're selling handmade items or vintage goods, add Etsy to the mix. Fashion sellers should consider Poshmark or Mercari alongside Swapedly. But for general secondhand goods with a mix of local and shipped sales, Swapedly hits the sweet spot.

    And the $40 new user bonus is an honest-to-goodness deal. See how Swapedly stacks up in our guide to the best Mercari alternatives and our full roundup of the best places to sell stuff online.
